summary: This lecture from the Zadul-Ma'ad series discusses the proper way to face the qibla during prayer. Sheikh Ali Mashhour explains the historical context of why Muslims initially prayed toward Bayt al-Maqdis before the command was changed to face toward the Kaaba in Mecca. He clarifies that scholars differ on the interpretation of facing the qibla—while some require precision toward the Kaaba's exact center, the majority opinion holds that facing the general direction is sufficient, with a 45-degree margin of acceptable deviation. The lecture emphasizes that intricate calculations are unnecessary and that historical mosques built centuries ago, many of which are slightly misaligned, demonstrate the validity of this understanding. The discussion then transitions to the length and content of Quranic recitation in different prayer times, noting that the Prophet would typically recite 60-100 medium-length verses in Fajr prayer, significantly more than contemporary practice.
suggested_title: Facing the Qibla in Prayer: Historical Context and Practical Application
catchy_title: How Precise Must You Face the Kaaba?
